1: Sarai, Abram’s wife, had borne him no children. But she had an Egyptian slave named Hagar — the human situation that sets up the test of faith: the promise of offspring (Genesis 12:2; Genesis 15:4–5) has not yet been fulfilled and Sarai has an Egyptian slave who could serve as a surrogate. Paul’s allegory in Galatians 4:21–31 makes Hagar/Sarah the two covenant lines: Hagar represents the Sinai covenant that produces children for slavery; Sarah represents the Jerusalem-above covenant that produces children of freedom. The Egyptian slave Hagar (Egypt = slavery in the canonical imagination) is the type of the law-covenant that produces bondage rather than freedom. This verse sets up the allegory’s starting point.
2: Sarai said, “The LORD has kept me from having children. Go, sleep with my slave; perhaps I can build a family through her” — the human attempt to fulfill God’s promise by natural means, bypassing divine timing. Paul in Galatians 4:23 calls Ishmael “born in the ordinary way” (lit. “according to the flesh”) in contrast to Isaac who was “born as the result of a divine promise.” The “flesh” path — achieving through human engineering what God has promised to give — is the theological trap that Sarai falls into and that the law represents in Paul’s allegory. Christ is not born “according to the flesh” by natural means; he is born of the Holy Spirit (Luke 1:35) — the supreme birth “according to the promise.”
3: After Abram had been living in Canaan ten years, Sarai gave Hagar to Abram — ten years of waiting for the promise followed by the human solution. The patience required for trusting the promise — ten years of waiting — is the Christological testing ground. Abram and Sarai’s inability to wait for God’s timing is the failure that the heroes of faith (Heb 11) mostly manage to overcome: “These all died in faith, not having received the things promised, but having seen them and greeted them from afar” (Hebrews 11:13). The ten-year wait that exceeds Sarai’s patience corresponds to the wait for Christ in which every generation of believers participates: “we wait for the blessed hope — the appearing of the glory of our great God and Savior, Jesus Christ” (Titus 2:13).
4: Hagar conceived and, when she knew she was pregnant, she began to despise her mistress — the human solution immediately produces conflict. Galatians 4:29: “at that time the son born according to the flesh persecuted the son born by the Spirit.” Paul applies this dynamic to the relationship between those who insist on law-keeping and those who live by the promise: the “flesh” route consistently persecutes the “Spirit” route. The conflict between Hagar and Sarai is the type of the conflict between the old covenant that produces slavery and the new covenant that produces freedom — the conflict that Paul addresses in his Galatian letter throughout. The human arrangement that bypasses God’s promise generates exactly the conflict it was meant to avoid.
5: Then Sarai said to Abram, “You are responsible for the wrong I am suffering. I put my slave in your arms, and now that she knows she is pregnant, she despises me” — the conflict between Sarai and Hagar that the human arrangement has produced. The irony is that Sarai devised the arrangement (v2) and now blames Abram for its consequences. The human attempt to fulfill God’s promise by means other than trust generates blame and conflict; the law-covenant produces the same dynamic (Romans 7:7–11: “when the commandment came, sin sprang to life”; “sin, seizing the opportunity afforded by the commandment, deceived me and through the commandment put me to death”). The law itself is good but produces conflict and blame rather than the peace that only the promise-faith relationship achieves.
6: Abram said, “Your slave is in your hands; do with her whatever you think best.” Then Sarai mistreated Hagar; so she fled — Hagar flees from mistreatment into the desert. The vulnerable slave fleeing from her mistress is the one whom God seeks out (v7–13). The God who seeks the fleeing, mistreated, and vulnerable is the type of Christ who leaves the ninety-nine to seek the one lost sheep (Luke 15:3–7), who seeks and saves the lost (Luke 19:10), who calls the weary and burdened to himself (Matthew 11:28). Hagar’s flight into the desert is answered by the Angel of the LORD finding her — divine initiative seeking out the abandoned and overlooked.
7: The angel of the LORD found Hagar near a spring in the desert — the first appearance of the “Angel of the LORD” (mal’ach YHWH) in Genesis. The Angel speaks in the first person as God (v10: “I will increase your descendants”) and is identified with the LORD by Hagar (v13: “She gave this name to the LORD who spoke to her”). The mainstream theological tradition identifies the Angel of the LORD as a pre-incarnate appearance of the Son — a theophany in which the second person of the Trinity appears in visible form before the incarnation. If so, this is Christ finding Hagar by the desert spring, just as he later finds the Samaritan woman at Jacob’s well (John 4:7) — seeking out the marginalized and overlooked at the place of water.
8: “Hagar, slave of Sarai, where have you come from, and where are you going?” — the Angel’s question is one of full knowing: the omniscient question that already knows the answer but draws out the person. The same pattern occurs in Christ’s questions: “What do you want me to do for you?” (Mark 10:51); “Woman, why are you crying? Who is it you are looking for?” (John 20:15). The divine question is not a request for information but an invitation to articulate one’s situation before God — the pastoral practice of the one who already sees all (v13: El Roi, “the God who sees me”). God’s questions draw people into self-disclosure before the one who already knows.
9: The angel of the LORD told her, “Go back to your mistress and submit to her” — the command to return is coupled with the promise (v10–11). The divine call back into a difficult situation with divine assurance is the pattern of much prophetic commissioning: Moses sent back to Egypt (Exod 3–4), Elijah sent back from Horeb (1 Kings 19:15). Christ himself returns from the wilderness to face his mission (Matthew 4:12–17); believers are sent back into the world rather than taken out of it (John 17:15–18: “My prayer is not that you take them out of the world but that you protect them from the evil one… As you sent me into the world, I have sent them into the world”). The call to return with divine promise is the shape of all divine commissioning.
10: The angel added, “I will increase your descendants so much that they will be too numerous to count” — the Angel speaks in the first person divine “I,” making a promise only God can make. This first-person divine speech by the Angel of the LORD is a primary argument for the theophanic identification: an angelic messenger would not speak as God (cf. Daniel 10:11–12, where the angel speaks in the third person about God). The promise to Hagar of innumerable descendants corresponds to the promise to Abraham (Genesis 13:16; Genesis 15:5), extended even to the servant and the son who is not the covenant heir. God’s generosity extends beyond the covenant line — a type of Christ’s mission beyond the house of Israel to “all nations” (Matthew 28:19).
11: “You are now pregnant and you will give birth to a son. You shall name him Ishmael, for the LORD has heard of your misery” — the name Ishmael (“God hears”) announces God’s character as the one who hears the afflicted. The divine hearing of Hagar’s misery connects to the canonical proclamation that God hears the cry of the poor and oppressed (Exodus 3:7: “I have heard them crying out… I am concerned about their suffering”; Psalms 34:17–18: “The righteous cry out, and the LORD hears them… he saves those who are crushed in spirit”). Christ is the fulfillment of the God who hears: “During the days of Jesus’ life on earth, he offered up prayers and petitions with fervent cries and tears to the one who could save him from death, and he was heard” (Hebrews 5:7). God hears both the suffering servant and those who cry to him through Christ.
12: “He will be a wild donkey of a man; his hand will be against everyone and everyone’s hand against him, and he will live in hostility toward all his brothers” — Ishmael’s character prophecy. This verse describes not Christ but the non-covenant line: Ishmael’s descendants live outside the specific covenant that runs through Isaac. The existence of the non-covenant line is itself a canonical-context note: God’s care for Hagar and Ishmael (vv10–11) shows that those outside the specific covenant line are not outside God’s general providence (Matthew 5:45: “he causes his sun to rise on the evil and the good”). Paul’s allegory in Galatians 4:29 notes that the “wild donkey” line persecutes the covenant line — the ongoing tension between human-arranged religion and promise-faith.
13: Hagar gave this name to the LORD who spoke to her: “You are El Roi, the God who sees me” — Hagar’s naming of God. She gives God a name based on her encounter: El Roi, “the God who sees.” This is remarkable for two reasons: (1) Hagar, a foreign slave woman, names God — no other OT figure names God; and (2) the God who sees is the same God who sees all suffering, vulnerability, and lostness. Christ embodies the God who sees: he sees the widow’s son being carried out for burial (Luke 7:13: “When the Lord saw her, his heart went out to her”); he sees Zacchaeus in the tree (Luke 19:5); he sees the woman bent double for eighteen years (Luke 13:12). El Roi is the God fully revealed in Christ’s attentiveness to the overlooked.
14: That is why the well was called Beer Lahai Roi (“well of the Living One who sees me”) — the naming of the well commemorates the divine-human encounter at the water. The well at which Hagar meets the God who sees her connects to the well at which Jesus meets the Samaritan woman (John 4:7–26): at the water, the divine visitor encounters the marginalized, speaks truth, and offers living water. Jesus offers “water welling up to eternal life” (John 4:14) — the living water that Beer Lahai Roi (“well of the living one”) foreshadows. Both scenes are desert-adjacent encounters at wells between a marginalized woman and the God who sees and knows her fully.
15: Hagar bore Abram a son, and Abram gave the name Ishmael to the son she had borne — the birth of Ishmael, the son born by human arrangement. Paul’s allegory (Galatians 4:22–24) reads the two births — Ishmael born by human effort, Isaac born by divine promise — as the two routes to standing before God: self-effort (which produces the slave) and faith-in-promise (which produces the heir). The birth of Ishmael is the “according to the flesh” route that Paul identifies with the Sinai covenant of the law; Isaac’s birth is “according to the Spirit.” Christ is the ultimate Isaac — born not by human will or a husband’s will, but born of God (John 1:13), the supreme birth by divine promise.
16: Abram was eighty-six years old when Hagar bore him Ishmael — the chronological marker. Abram is 86 when Ishmael is born; he will be 99 at the circumcision covenant (Genesis 17:1) and 100 when Isaac is born (Genesis 21:5). The fourteen-year gap between Ishmael’s birth and Isaac’s is the fourteen years in which the human arrangement seems to be the fulfillment of the promise, until God reveals the true heir. The waiting period between the human substitute and the true promise — fourteen years in the narrative, centuries in the broader canon — is the structure of redemptive history: the law as the substitute that managed sin until the promise was fulfilled in Christ (Galatians 3:19: the law “was added because of transgressions until the Seed to whom the promise referred had come”).
